name: cards-stack
description: Parallel items stacked vertically, full-width cards.
purpose: Use when the items want vertical reading order — sequential exploration rather than a-glance comparison. 2–3 items work best.

cards.description: Each list item becomes one stacked card. Authoring contract: a top-level bullet is the card title (renders bold by default); an indented bullet underneath carries the body text.

skeleton:
<!-- _class: cards-stack -->

## Slide heading.

- First card title
  - Body text for the first stacked card, two short sentences max.
- Second card title
  - Body text for the second stacked card.
- Third card title
  - Body text for the third stacked card.
sample:
<!-- _class: cards-stack -->

## Three forces are compressing the differentiation window.

- Infrastructure has commoditized.
  - The platform work that took us two years is now a managed service a competitor can switch on in an afternoon. The moat is no longer the stack.
- Release cycles have collapsed.
  - What used to ship annually now ships monthly. A visible advantage is matched before the next board meeting.
- Switching costs are rising.
  - Customers consolidate vendors and sign longer contracts. The window to win an account is shorter, and losing one lasts longer.

whenToUse.title: Vertical reading order
whenToUse.body: The audience scans top-to-bottom, not grid-style. Use when each card builds on the previous one as the eye moves down the slide.

whenToUse.title: Two sentences per card
whenToUse.body: More body than cards-grid can hold without crowding. cards-stack lets each card carry one or two short sentences without losing the layout balance.

whenToUse.title: Two or three items
whenToUse.body: Sweet spot is three. Past that the slide overflows — split across multiple slides or switch to cards-grid with shorter body text per card.

antiPatterns.title: Four or more items
antiPatterns.body: The stack overflows past three. For four parallel items reach for cards-grid four; for richer per-item bodies, cards-wide handles three or four rows.

antiPatterns.title: One-line cards
antiPatterns.body: If each card is a single short phrase, the stack reads as a padded list. Drop to `list` or `tldr` and reclaim the vertical space.

antiPatterns.title: Forced sequence
antiPatterns.body: Cards-stack is parallel content read in vertical order, not a numbered sequence. For explicit steps, use list-steps or list-criteria.

related.name: cards-grid
related.when: three or four parallel items in a scannable grid

related.name: cards-wide
related.when: three or four rows with more substantial per-card body

related.name: cards-side
related.when: exactly two items in left-right balance

related.name: list-steps
related.when: items carry an explicit, ordered sequence

horizontal.label: Horizontal cards
horizontal.caption: Stacked rows pivot to a left-aligned title column with the body to its right — useful when the card titles are short labels and the body carries the weight.
horizontal.sample:
<!-- _class: cards-stack horizontal -->

## Three patterns, each with its own pull.

- Inventory.
  - Equal-weight items the audience scans without ordering. The cards-grid family lives here — grid, stack, wide, side.
- Comparison.
  - Two or more items weighed against shared criteria. The verdict and compare families live here — they take sides.
- Progression.
  - Items that carry an explicit sequence. The list-steps and timeline families live here — order is load-bearing.

numbered.label: Numbered stack
numbered.caption: Authored as `ol` (`1.` source). Each row carries a flush corner number — use when the stack carries an implicit count ("three options", "four phases") even if the order is interchangeable.
numbered.sample:
<!-- _class: cards-stack -->

## Three reasons to keep cards-stack at three items.

1. Cognitive load
   - Three is the threshold the audience can hold without effort. Past three, the slide demands working memory the room shouldn't have to spend.
2. Vertical real estate
   - Each stacked card needs ~30% of the slide height to breathe. Four cards force you to shrink the cards until they stop reading as cards.
3. Build path symmetry
   - cards-stack pairs with cards-grid (3-4 items) and cards-wide (3-4 rows). Keeping cards-stack at 2-3 keeps the family's choices clean.
